stories.title: One More Step
stories.body:
There is a quiet moment in every race when the body starts asking questions. Why continue? Why push harder? Why not slow down and accept that today is not the day? For many runners, this moment comes long before the finish line. It arrives when the legs feel heavy, when breathing becomes sharp, and when the distance ahead still looks too long.

But running has a special way of teaching courage. It does not always reward the fastest person. Sometimes it rewards the one who refuses to stop. One more step becomes one more chance. One more breath becomes one more reason to believe. The finish line may look far away, but every small movement brings it closer.

That is why a race is never only about speed. It is about the promise a person makes to themselves before the start. It is about showing up, even when doubt is louder than confidence. It is about discovering that strength is not always loud, dramatic, or visible. Sometimes strength is simply continuing when stopping would be easier.

The most powerful victories are not always measured by medals. Sometimes the real victory is crossing the line knowing you did not give up on yourself. And that kind of finish stays with you long after the race is over.

stories.title: The Final Minute
stories.body:
The final minute of a football match can feel longer than the entire game. The score is close, the crowd is loud, and every pass carries pressure. Players who were tired moments ago suddenly find one last burst of energy. A single mistake can change everything, but so can a single brave decision.

In those final seconds, football becomes more than a game of tactics. It becomes a test of trust. A defender trusts the goalkeeper. A midfielder trusts the forward. A player with the ball trusts the teammate making a run no one else has noticed. The best moments often happen when a team keeps believing, even when the clock says there is almost no time left.

A great comeback does not begin with the final goal. It begins much earlier, in training sessions, in difficult losses, in moments when the team learns how to stay together under pressure. The final minute only reveals what has already been built.

That is why football memories can feel so powerful. A late goal, a saved shot, a team celebration, or even a painful defeat can become part of someone's story. The scoreboard shows the result, but the heart remembers the effort.

stories.title: The Shot After the Miss
stories.body:
Every basketball player remembers a missed shot. The ball leaves the hand, the eyes follow it, and for a second everything feels possible. Then it hits the rim, bounces away, and the moment is gone. In a close game, one miss can feel heavy enough to carry for the rest of the night.

But basketball does not allow a player to live in one mistake for too long. The game keeps moving. There is another possession, another pass, another chance to defend, another chance to shoot. That is one of the most important lessons the sport can teach: mistakes are part of the rhythm, not the end of the story.

The player who becomes stronger is not the one who never misses. It is the one who learns how to take the next shot with a clear mind. Confidence is not pretending the miss never happened. Confidence is understanding that one moment does not define the entire game.

A great performance is often built from recovery. A missed shot can lead to better focus. A turnover can lead to stronger defense. A difficult quarter can lead to a powerful comeback. Basketball rewards movement, courage, and the ability to reset quickly.

That is why the next shot matters. Not because it erases the miss, but because it proves the player is still present, still brave, and still ready.

stories.title: The Silence Under Water
stories.body:
Swimming is one of the few sports where the world becomes quiet. Above the water there may be noise, voices, and expectations, but under the surface there is only movement, breath, and focus. Every stroke has a rhythm. Every turn matters. Every second is earned.

For a swimmer, progress is often invisible to others. It happens in early mornings, repeated laps, tired shoulders, and countless attempts to improve by a fraction of a second. The difference between disappointment and celebration can be incredibly small, yet the work behind that difference is enormous.

Water teaches patience. It does not respond to force alone. A swimmer must learn balance, timing, control, and calm. Panic wastes energy. Focus creates speed. The best races often come from the athlete who can stay steady when everything inside wants to rush.

A personal best in swimming is not just a number on a screen. It is proof that small improvements matter. It is proof that discipline can turn into confidence. It is proof that even in silence, a person can fight hard for something meaningful.

Every lap tells a story. Some are difficult, some are slow, some feel impossible, but together they build the swimmer who touches the wall stronger than before.

stories.title: The Point That Changed Everything
stories.body:
In tennis, a match can turn on one point. It may not look important at first. The rally begins like any other: serve, return, movement, patience. But then something shifts. One player chooses courage instead of safety. One difficult shot lands inside the line. One moment of belief changes the energy of the match.

Tennis is a lonely sport in a beautiful and difficult way. There is no teammate to take the next shot. No one can run for you, decide for you, or calm your thoughts between points. The player must carry the pressure, the plan, and the emotion alone.

That is why mental strength matters so much. A tennis player can lose a point and still win the match. They can lose a set and still find a way back. They can feel nervous and still play with courage. The score may change quickly, but the most important battle often happens inside the player's mind.

A comeback in tennis is not just about hitting better. It is about refusing to let the past point control the next one. It is about starting again, over and over, until the match is truly finished.

The point that changes everything is not always the most spectacular one. Sometimes it is simply the point where the player decides to believe again.

stories.title: The Hill Ahead
stories.body:
Every cyclist knows the feeling of seeing a hill ahead. From far away, it looks still and silent, but the body already understands what is coming. The road rises, the speed drops, and every turn of the pedals demands more effort. A hill does not care how tired you are. It only asks one question: will you keep climbing?

Cycling teaches a powerful kind of persistence. Progress can feel slow on a difficult climb, but slow progress is still progress. The rider may not feel fast, but they are moving forward. Meter by meter, breath by breath, the top becomes closer.

The climb is also a test of pacing. Push too hard too early, and the hill becomes heavier. Give up too soon, and you never discover what was waiting after the hardest part. The best riders learn to respect the challenge, control their energy, and trust the rhythm.

Then comes the moment when the road finally levels out. The pressure fades, the view opens, and the rider understands something important: the climb was not only an obstacle. It was the reason the finish felt meaningful.

In sport, as in life, some paths are steep because they are leading somewhere worth reaching.

stories.title: The Bell Rings Again
stories.body:
In boxing, the sound of the bell can mean many things. It can bring relief at the end of a difficult round. It can signal the start of another challenge. It can give a fighter a few seconds to breathe, listen, and decide who they will be when they stand up again.

A boxing match is not only about power. It is about control, patience, and the ability to stay focused while under pressure. A fighter must think clearly when tired, protect themselves when emotions rise, and keep moving even after a hard hit.

One of the most inspiring parts of boxing is not the punch that lands, but the moment after a fighter is tested. Do they lose focus, or do they adjust? Do they rush, or do they return with discipline? The strongest fighters are not fearless. They are trained to act with courage even when fear is present.

Between rounds, everything becomes simple. Breathe. Listen. Reset. The past round cannot be changed, but the next one has not been written yet. That small pause can become the beginning of a comeback.

The bell rings again, and the fighter steps forward. Not because the fight is easy, but because they are still willing to face it.

stories.title: The Team That Refused to Fall
stories.body:
Volleyball is a sport of quick reactions and shared responsibility. One player receives, another sets, another attacks, and the whole team moves as one. When the ball is in the air, everyone has a role. No point belongs to only one person.

A difficult match can test a team's spirit. A few lost points can create pressure. A mistake can spread doubt. But volleyball also gives a team something powerful: the chance to recover together. A player who misses a serve can be encouraged by a teammate. A blocked attack can be followed by a better setup. A lost set can become the beginning of a stronger response.

The best volleyball teams are not perfect. They communicate, adjust, and support each other when the game becomes tense. Their strength is not only in jumping higher or hitting harder. It is in staying connected.

A great rally shows the beauty of the sport. The ball is saved when it seems impossible, passed with control, set with trust, and finished with confidence. In that moment, the team becomes more than separate players. It becomes one moving idea.

That is why volleyball victories feel so collective. They are built from voices, effort, trust, and the refusal to let the ball fall without a fight.

stories.title: The First Clean Performance
stories.body:
A clean figure skating performance can look effortless from the outside. The music begins, the skater moves across the ice, and every jump, spin, and step seems to flow naturally. But behind that smooth performance are countless falls, repeated corrections, tired legs, and days when confidence feels far away.

Ice remembers everything. It remembers the first nervous attempt, the jump that did not land, the turn that felt impossible, and the practice session when everything finally started to connect. A skater learns that beauty is not created by avoiding difficulty. It is created by working through it until movement becomes expression.

The most emotional moment often comes near the end of the program. The skater knows they are close. One more element, one more breath, one more controlled landing. The audience may see grace, but the skater feels the weight of every hour that brought them there.

When the final pose arrives and the performance is clean, it is more than a score. It is a memory of patience becoming confidence. It is proof that falling many times does not mean failure. Sometimes it means the body is learning how to fly.

On the ice, every clean performance is a quiet celebration of everything no one saw.

stories.title: The Medal No One Sees
stories.body:
People often notice the final moment first. The medal, the trophy, the celebration, the photo, the applause. It is easy to think that this is where the story lives. But in sport, the most important parts often happen far away from the spotlight.

The real story may begin on a cold morning when training feels difficult. It may continue through small injuries, lost competitions, missed chances, and days when progress seems too slow. It may include doubts that no one hears and sacrifices that no one posts online.

That is why every visible achievement has an invisible side. The medal is only the symbol. The true victory is the discipline behind it. The routine. The patience. The decision to keep improving when no one is watching.

Not every athlete receives a medal, but every athlete can earn a moment of pride. Finishing a first race, returning after a break, learning a new skill, helping a teammate, or simply becoming better than yesterday can all become meaningful victories.

Sport is not only about standing on the podium. It is about becoming someone who knows how to work, wait, try again, and respect the journey. The medal people see may shine for a moment, but the strength built along the way lasts much longer.

stories.title: The Try That Started With Trust
stories.body:
A rugby try rarely begins at the moment the player crosses the line. It begins much earlier, with a pass made under pressure, a teammate running into space, a defender choosing the right angle, and a group of players trusting the plan even when the field looks chaotic. From the outside, rugby can look like pure strength, but inside the game there is timing, communication, and deep trust.

Every player carries responsibility. One person may gain ground, but another protects the ball. One player may make the final run, but others created the space. A powerful moment on the scoreboard is often the result of many smaller decisions made by the whole team. Rugby teaches that success is rarely a solo achievement.

The most inspiring teams are the ones that keep moving forward after contact. They do not stop because the path is blocked. They adjust, support, and continue. The ball moves from hand to hand, and with every pass, the team says the same thing without words: we are still in this together.

That is why a try can feel so meaningful. It is not only about reaching the line. It is about the courage to keep advancing, the discipline to stay connected, and the belief that progress is possible even when every meter is difficult.

stories.title: The Weight That Became a Goal
stories.body:
A heavy barbell can look simple from a distance. It is metal, plates, and numbers. But for an athlete, that weight can become a story. It can represent a goal that once felt impossible, a personal limit that needed to be challenged, or a quiet promise made during training.

Weightlifting is honest. The bar does not care about excuses, mood, or reputation. It only responds to preparation, technique, focus, and strength built over time. Some days the lift feels smooth. Other days the same weight feels heavier than expected. That is part of the process.

Progress in weightlifting is often slow, but every small improvement matters. A better position, a stronger grip, a cleaner movement, or one more kilogram on the bar can become a real victory. The athlete learns that strength is not created in one dramatic moment. It is built through repetition, recovery, patience, and respect for the body.

The best lift is not always the heaviest one. Sometimes it is the lift that proves the athlete has grown. It is the moment when fear becomes focus, when doubt becomes effort, and when the weight that once looked impossible finally leaves the ground.

stories.title: The Race Against Yesterday
stories.body:
In athletics, the opponent is not always the person in the next lane. Sometimes the real opponent is yesterday's version of yourself. The old time, the old distance, the old hesitation, the old fear. Every training session becomes a quiet conversation with who you were and who you are trying to become.

A runner may chase seconds. A jumper may chase centimeters. A thrower may chase one cleaner release. These numbers can seem small, but in sport they carry meaning. They show effort becoming visible. They show that the body is adapting and the mind is learning patience.

There are days when progress feels invisible. The stopwatch may not show a personal best. The result may not match the work. But athletics teaches that improvement does not always arrive in a straight line. Sometimes the body is building strength before the result appears.

The race against yesterday is powerful because it never truly ends. Every athlete has another chance to step onto the track, into the field, or toward the line with a little more experience than before. Victory can mean winning the event, but it can also mean showing up stronger, calmer, and braver than last time.

stories.title: The Wall Above
stories.body:
At the bottom of a climbing wall, the route can look impossible. The holds are small, the height feels serious, and the path is not always obvious. But climbers learn to study the wall differently. They do not only see the challenge. They search for the next move.

Climbing is a sport of problem-solving with the whole body. Strength helps, but patience matters just as much. A climber must decide where to place a hand, how to shift weight, when to trust a foothold, and when to rest. One wrong movement can make the route harder, but one calm decision can open the way upward.

The most inspiring part of climbing is that progress happens one hold at a time. The top may be far away, but the climber does not need to solve everything at once. They only need to find the next secure position. Then the next. Then the next.

That is why climbing feels like a lesson in courage. The wall does not become smaller, but the climber becomes more prepared. Fear may still be there, but focus becomes stronger. And when the top is finally reached, the view is not only a reward. It is proof that impossible can become possible through patience, trust, and steady effort.

stories.title: The Trick After a Hundred Falls
stories.body:
A skateboard trick can last less than a second, but learning it can take days, weeks, or months. From the outside, the final success looks effortless. The board flips, the feet land, and the rider rolls away. But behind that clean moment are falls, bruises, frustration, and hundreds of attempts that almost worked.

Skateboarding has a unique relationship with failure. Falling is not unusual. It is part of the language of the sport. Every missed landing teaches balance. Every awkward attempt teaches timing. Every fall asks the rider whether the trick still matters enough to try again.

The most powerful moment is often not the landing itself, but the decision before it. The skater stands there, breathes, looks at the obstacle or the flat ground, and chooses to commit one more time. That decision is where courage lives.

When the trick finally lands, it feels bigger than movement. It feels like proof. Proof that persistence has value. Proof that progress can be messy. Proof that confidence is sometimes built by failing in public and still returning to the board.

Skateboarding reminds us that style is not only how someone moves. It is also how they recover, how they laugh after a fall, and how they keep chasing the trick that once seemed out of reach.

stories.title: The Save in the Last Second
stories.body:
A hockey game can change in the blink of an eye. The puck moves fast, skates cut across the ice, and decisions happen almost before the mind can explain them. For a goalkeeper, every second carries pressure. One shot can decide the game. One save can become unforgettable.

The last-second save is one of the most dramatic moments in sport. The crowd rises, the attacker shoots, and for an instant everything is uncertain. The goalkeeper reacts with instinct built from thousands of repetitions. A glove, a pad, a stretch across the crease — and suddenly the chance is gone.

But that save did not appear from nowhere. It was created in lonely practice sessions, in repeated drills, in mistakes that became lessons, and in the ability to stay focused when tired. Goalkeepers must learn not only movement, but memory. They study angles, read body language, and prepare for moments that may last less than a second.

A great save is more than defense. It gives a team belief. It says the game is not over. It gives everyone one more chance to fight, skate, pass, and score.

That is why the goalkeeper's moment can feel so heroic. They may stand alone in the crease, but one brave save can carry the hope of the whole team.

stories.title: The Breath Before Release
stories.body:
Archery is a sport where silence becomes powerful. The archer stands still, but inside that stillness there is focus, discipline, and control. The target waits in the distance. The bow is drawn. The body must remain steady, and the mind must become calm enough to let the arrow go.

The most important moment is often the breath before release. Too much tension can disturb the shot. Too much hurry can break the rhythm. The archer must trust training, posture, and patience. The goal is not to force the arrow, but to guide the moment.

Archery teaches that accuracy is not only about aiming. It is about controlling emotion. A missed shot can create frustration, but the next arrow still deserves a clear mind. A perfect shot can create excitement, but the next one still requires discipline. Every arrow is a new beginning.

There is something inspiring about a sport where small movements matter so much. A tiny adjustment can change the result. A calm breath can become the difference between disappointment and pride.

When the arrow flies, it carries more than strength. It carries concentration, timing, and trust. And whether it lands in the center or not, the archer learns again how powerful focus can be.

stories.title: The Rally That Would Not End
stories.body:
A table tennis rally can become a tiny storm. The ball moves quickly, spins sharply, and demands instant reaction. To someone watching, it may look playful and fast. To the players, every shot is a calculation made in a fraction of a second.

The longest rallies are not only tests of speed. They test patience, control, and nerve. A player must decide whether to attack, defend, change the spin, or wait for a better chance. The smallest hesitation can lose the point. The smallest adjustment can win it.

What makes table tennis exciting is how quickly momentum can change. A player who is under pressure can turn the point around with one smart placement. A risky shot can become a brilliant winner. A defensive return can buy enough time to rebuild confidence.

The rally that would not end becomes memorable because both players refuse to give the point away. They move, reach, adjust, and answer again and again. It becomes less about one spectacular hit and more about persistence under speed.

When the point finally ends, everyone understands that something special happened. Not because it lasted long, but because it showed the beauty of focus: quick thinking, steady hands, and the will to stay in the moment.

stories.title: The Partnership at Full Speed
stories.body:
Equestrian sport is different from many other sports because success depends on two athletes moving as one. The rider may guide, but the horse must trust. The horse may have strength, but the rider must communicate with calm precision. Together, they create a partnership built through time.

At full speed, there is no room for panic. A jump approaches, the distance must be judged, and the rhythm must stay balanced. The rider's body speaks through small signals. The horse responds with power and confidence. When everything works, the movement looks natural, almost effortless.

But harmony does not appear instantly. It is built through daily care, training, patience, and respect. The best partnerships are not based only on control. They are based on understanding. A rider learns the horse's energy, fears, habits, and strengths. The horse learns to trust the rider's guidance.

That is why a clean round or a graceful performance feels deeply emotional. It is not just a technical result. It is proof of connection. It shows what can happen when communication becomes quiet, strong, and honest.

In equestrian sport, victory is shared. Every successful moment belongs to both hearts moving toward the same goal.

stories.title: The Lesson in Respect
stories.body:
Martial arts often begin with movement, but they grow through respect. A student learns how to strike, block, fall, recover, and defend. Yet the deeper lesson is not only about winning a fight. It is about discipline, self-control, patience, and understanding personal responsibility.

In training, progress is visible in small ways. A movement becomes cleaner. Balance improves. Reactions become calmer. A student who once felt nervous begins to stand with more confidence. Every belt, rank, or achievement is not just a symbol of skill. It represents time, effort, and character.

One of the most powerful parts of martial arts is learning how to control strength. True ability is not shown by acting carelessly. It is shown by knowing when to use power, when to hold back, and how to respect an opponent. The strongest athlete is often the one with the calmest mind.

There will always be difficult sessions. There will be mistakes, tired muscles, and techniques that take a long time to understand. But the training teaches that growth requires humility. No one becomes great by refusing to learn.

Martial arts remind us that sport can shape more than the body. It can shape confidence, focus, and the way a person carries themselves in the world.

stories.title: The Quiet Shot
stories.body:
Golf is a sport where a single shot can feel enormous. The field is open, the target is far away, and the mind has plenty of time to think. That is what makes the sport so challenging. The body may know the movement, but the mind must stay steady enough to let it happen.

A golfer learns that power is only part of the game. Control, patience, and decision-making matter just as much. Sometimes the smartest shot is not the longest one. Sometimes the best choice is to stay safe, recover, and prepare for the next opportunity.

The quiet before a shot can be intense. There is no running clock, no loud contact, no immediate rush. The athlete stands alone with the club, the distance, the wind, and the decision. In that silence, confidence must come from preparation.

Golf teaches that mistakes are part of the course. A bad shot does not end the round unless the player allows it to control the next one. The strongest players are able to reset, study the situation, and continue with patience.

A great shot is beautiful because it combines skill and calm. It travels through the air as the result of focus, practice, and trust. And sometimes, one quiet shot can change the entire round.

stories.title: The Routine That Held Its Breath
stories.body:
A gymnastics routine can feel like a moment suspended in the air. The athlete runs, jumps, twists, balances, and lands with precision. The audience sees beauty and strength, but the gymnast feels every detail: the grip, the timing, the landing, the breath.

Gymnastics demands bravery in a unique way. The body must move through space with trust and control. A small hesitation can change the rhythm. A small correction can save the routine. Every element requires repetition until the athlete can perform under pressure.

Behind a clean routine are years of practice. There are falls no one remembers, corrections repeated again and again, and moments when a skill seemed too difficult. Gymnasts learn early that progress is built from patience and resilience.

The final landing often carries the most emotion. Feet hit the mat, arms rise, and for a second the athlete knows whether the routine held together. That moment can bring relief, pride, or tears, because it contains everything that came before it.

Gymnastics is inspiring because it shows what disciplined courage can look like. It is power made graceful, fear made controlled, and hard work turned into movement.

stories.title: The Current and the Paddle
stories.body:
On the water, an athlete quickly learns that control does not mean forcing everything. The current has its own direction, speed, and mood. A canoeist or kayaker must work with the water, not against it. Every paddle stroke becomes a conversation between strength and timing.

Some races happen on calm water, where rhythm matters most. Others happen in rough conditions, where reaction and courage are tested. In both cases, the athlete must stay focused. The boat responds to balance, angle, and precision. A rushed movement can waste energy. A smart one can create speed.

Water sports teach adaptability. Conditions can change quickly. Wind may rise, waves may shift, and the athlete must continue making decisions. The goal is not to control the water completely. The goal is to understand it well enough to move forward.

The most inspiring moment often comes when the athlete finds rhythm. The paddle enters the water, the boat glides, and effort turns into motion. The body works hard, but the movement begins to feel connected and alive.

Every journey on water is a reminder that progress is not always about fighting harder. Sometimes it is about learning the current, adjusting with patience, and trusting each stroke.

stories.title: The Comeback No One Expected
stories.body:
A comeback begins when the result looks almost decided. The score is against the team, time is running out, and many people watching may already believe the ending is clear. But the players on the field, court, or ice still have a choice. They can accept the story, or they can try to rewrite it.

The first step of a comeback is often small. One good pass. One strong defensive play. One point. One saved chance. It may not look like much, but it changes the feeling. The team begins to believe again. The crowd senses energy returning. The opponent feels pressure that was not there before.

Comebacks are powerful because they reveal character. It is easy to play with confidence when everything is going well. It is much harder to stay united when mistakes have already happened. A team that fights back shows trust, patience, and courage under pressure.

Not every comeback ends with victory, but every real comeback changes something. It proves that effort still matters when the situation is difficult. It proves that time left on the clock still has value. It proves that a team can become stronger by refusing to disappear.

That is why unexpected comebacks stay in memory. They remind us that in sport, the ending is not finished until the final whistle, buzzer, or line.

facts.text: Running can improve endurance gradually because the heart, lungs, and muscles adapt to repeated effort over time.
A standard football match usually lasts 90 minutes, but added time can make the final moments even more intense and unpredictable.
Basketball was invented in 1891 by James Naismith as an indoor game to keep students active during winter.
Swimming uses almost the entire body, which is why it is often considered one of the most complete physical activities.
In tennis, the term "love" means zero, and it is one of the most recognizable scoring words in sport.
Cyclists often use drafting, riding close behind another cyclist, to reduce air resistance and save energy.
Boxing footwork is just as important as punches, because movement helps fighters control distance, balance, and timing.
A volleyball team can touch the ball up to three times before sending it over the net, usually with a pass, set, and attack.
Figure skaters combine athletic jumps, spins, balance, and musical expression in one performance.
Many athletes use visualization before competitions, imagining their performance to build focus and confidence.
In rugby, a "try" is scored when a player grounds the ball in the opponent's in-goal area.
Proper technique in weightlifting is essential because lifting safely depends on body position, timing, and control.
In sprinting, reaction time at the start can strongly affect the final result, especially in short-distance races.
Climbing is not only about strength; balance, route planning, grip technique, and calm decision-making are just as important.
Many skateboard tricks are built from small variations of balance, timing, board control, and body rotation.
Ice hockey players can change direction extremely quickly because skate blades allow sharp turns and rapid acceleration.
In archery, breathing control helps the archer stay steady before releasing the arrow.
Table tennis balls can spin very fast, making timing and paddle angle important for every return.
Equestrian sport depends on communication between rider and horse, using balance, posture, reins, and subtle body signals.
Martial arts often focus on discipline, respect, balance, and self-control, not only physical techniques.
Golf courses are designed with different challenges such as bunkers, slopes, wind, water hazards, and distance control.
Gymnasts need strength, flexibility, coordination, and body awareness to perform complex movements safely.
In canoeing and kayaking, paddle angle and rhythm can strongly affect speed, balance, and direction.
Strong communication can change the outcome of a team sport because players must react together under pressure.
Field hockey players use only one flat side of the stick to control, pass, and shoot the ball.
Snowboarders shift body weight between the front and back foot to control turns, speed, and balance.
A badminton shuttlecock can travel extremely fast after a smash, making reaction time very important.
Curling players sweep the ice to influence the speed and direction of the stone.
In motorsport, pit stops can decide races because even a few seconds gained or lost can change track position.
Rest and recovery are part of athletic progress because muscles need time to repair and become stronger.
